posts.content: In this incredibly detailed and gracious review of “The Irrational Decision,” @himself.bsky.social  situates the book in the web of intellectual history and the war between the AI enthusiasts and skeptics.

In lieu of lecture blogging on simulation and prediction, check out this fortuitously timed op-ed in the NY Times by @leifw.bsky.social and me on the absurdity of Silicon Sampling. (Related lecture blog tomorrow!)
A little more than a decade ago, a bunch of universities decided they needed to start schools of computing. What are the great success stories of this movement?
Pulling from this week’s lecture and op-ed on silicon sampling, I map the territory of simulation and its many purposes.
Solving the game of prediction with calibration and accounting strategies: A lecture blog post on defensive forecasting.
I understand and agree with Quinn flagging this as idiotic, but I have seen countless academic papers bragging about similar approaches in their methods sections.
I tried to pin down what people mean by "simulation" and place it in the broader context of the study and design of feedback systems.
The long legacy of simulation in control theory and what it can teach us about transferring policies from GPU to reality.
A high-level introduction to the "structured singular value" and what it teaches us about the art of uncertainty quantification.
Identifying the elements of a theory of engineering architecture.
Building a theory of the architecture of organizing machines and people.
Assembling a reading list for a class on the theory of engineering architecture.
To my Madison people: I’ll be talking about The Irrational Decision at 12:30 tomorrow at the Wisconsin Institute for Discovery. Would be great to see you there. silo.wisc.edu/talk/2026-04...

xPosts.content: For the first time in almost a decade, I'm teaching a class on learning and control.
xPosts.highlights: Ben Recht is returning to teaching a course on learning and control after nearly ten years.
xPosts.worthReading: Shows his ongoing academic engagement and focus on the intersection of learning and control systems.

xPosts.content: Part one of a new blog series: using the discovery of vitamins as a parable for why replication crises in [science/ML] matter.
xPosts.highlights: Introduces a blog series using the history of vitamin discovery as an analogy to discuss the importance of replication in scientific and machine learning research.
xPosts.worthReading: Applies a historical scientific case to critique modern research practices, emphasizing methodological rigor.

xPosts.content: Revisiting Sutton's Bitter Lesson in the wake of GPT-5.
xPosts.highlights: Re-examines the 'Bitter Lesson'—the idea that general methods leveraging computation scale best—in the context of advanced models like GPT-5.
xPosts.worthReading: Engages with a foundational AI debate about scaling vs. specialized knowledge in the era of large language models.

interestSummary: Ben Recht is currently focused on the theory of engineering architecture, simulation, and feedback systems, as well as teaching a class on learning and control, while also engaging with critiques of AI hype and replication crises in machine learning.
